title: Fare Well
content:
When I lie where shades of darkness
 Shall no more assail mine eyes,
 Nor the rain make lamentation
     When the wind sighs;
 How will fare the world whose wonder
 Was the very proof of me?
 Memory fades, must the remembered
     Perishing be?

 Oh, when this my dust surrenders
 Hand, foot, lip, to dust again,
 May these loved and loving faces
     Please other men!
 May the rustling harvest hedgerow
 Still the Traveller's Joy entwine,
 And as happy children gather
     Posies once mine.

 Look thy last on all things lovely,
 Every hour. Let no night
 Seal thy sense in deathly slumber
     Till to delight
 Thou have paid thy utmost blessing;
 Since that all things thou wouldst praise
 Beauty took from those who loved them
     In other days.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Walter De La Mare, titled "Fare Well", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
